worked_example: Fitting a line through $(1,1),(2,2),(3,2)$ leads to an overdetermined system solved in the least-squares sense, minimizing the sum of squared vertical residuals.

short_description: The unique matrix A^+ generalizing the inverse to any (including non-square or rank-deficient) matrix, giving the minimum-norm least-squares solution of Ax = b.
definition: For an m x n matrix A, the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse A^+ is the unique n x m matrix satisfying the four Penrose conditions: A A^+ A = A, A^+ A A^+ = A^+, (A A^+)^T = A A^+, and (A^+ A)^T = A^+ A. It can be computed from the singular value decomposition A = U Sigma V^T as A^+ = V Sigma^+ U^T, where Sigma^+ is formed by transposing Sigma and inverting its nonzero singular values. The vector x = A^+ b is the least-squares solution of Ax = b of smallest Euclidean norm. When A is invertible, A^+ = A^{-1}; when A has full column rank, A^+ = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T.
formula_latex: A = U \Sigma V^{T} \;\Rightarrow\; A^{+} = V \Sigma^{+} U^{T}, \qquad x = A^{+} b
formula_plaintext: If A = U Sigma V^T then A^+ = V Sigma^+ U^T; x = A^+ b is the minimum-norm least-squares solution
examples: For a full-column-rank A, A^+ = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T (the left inverse).
For a full-row-rank A, A^+ = A^T (A A^T)^{-1} (the right inverse).
For an invertible square A, A^+ = A^{-1}.
worked_example: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$, $A^+ = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T = \tfrac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

short_description: The linear system A^T A x = A^T b whose solutions are exactly the least-squares solutions of the (possibly overdetermined) system Ax = b.
statement: The least-squares solutions of Ax = b, i.e. the minimizers of ||Ax - b||_2, are precisely the solutions of the normal equations A^T A x = A^T b. If A has full column rank then A^T A is invertible (symmetric positive definite) and the least-squares solution is unique: x = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b.
formula_plaintext: A^T A x = A^T b
derivation: Minimizing f(x) = ||Ax - b||_2^2 = (Ax-b)^T(Ax-b), set the gradient to zero: grad f = 2 A^T (Ax - b) = 0, giving A^T A x = A^T b. Equivalently, the residual b - Ax must be orthogonal to the column space of A, i.e. A^T (b - Ax) = 0.
conditions: Holds for any real A and b; solutions always exist since A^T b lies in the column space of A^T A.
A^T A is invertible (unique solution) if and only if A has full column rank (linearly independent columns).
Numerically the normal equations can be ill-conditioned (condition number squared); QR or SVD is often preferred.
worked_example: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, $b = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$, the normal equations $A^T A x = A^T b$ give the best-fit line coefficients.

short_description: The Kronecker product of two matrices forms a larger block matrix whose blocks are scalar multiples of the second matrix, encoding tensor products of linear maps.
formula_latex: A \otimes B = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11}B & \cdots & a_{1n}B \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_{m1}B & \cdots & a_{mn}B \end{bmatrix}
formula_plaintext: A ⊗ B = [ a_ij * B ] (block matrix of size (m p) x (n q) when A is m x n and B is p x q)
definition: For A an m×n matrix and B a p×q matrix, the Kronecker product A ⊗ B is the mp×nq block matrix whose (i,j) block is a_{ij}B. It represents the tensor product of the two linear maps in the standard bases.
domain_of_definition: Any pair of matrices A (m×n) and B (p×q) over a common field; no size compatibility is required.
worked_example: For $A=\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{bmatrix}$ and $B=\begin{bmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}$, $A\otimes B=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0&2\\1&0&2&0\\0&3&0&4\\3&0&4&0\end{bmatrix}$.

references.title: Horn & Johnson, Topics in Matrix Analysis, Ch. 4

short_description: The outer product of two vectors is the matrix u v^T; it is a rank-1 matrix (when nonzero) and is the building block of low-rank decompositions.
formula_latex: u v^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} u_1 v_1 & \cdots & u_1 v_n \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ u_m v_1 & \cdots & u_m v_n \end{bmatrix}, \qquad (u v^{T})_{ij} = u_i v_j
formula_plaintext: u v^T has (i,j) entry u_i * v_j, giving an m x n matrix from u in R^m and v in R^n
definition: Given column vectors u ∈ R^m and v ∈ R^n, the outer product is the m×n matrix u v^T with entries (u v^T)_{ij} = u_i v_j. If u and v are both nonzero, u v^T has rank exactly 1, and every rank-1 matrix arises this way.
domain_of_definition: Any vectors u ∈ R^m (or C^m) and v ∈ R^n; for complex vectors the conjugate transpose u v^* is often used.
worked_example: With $u=(1,2,3)^T$ and $v=(4,5)^T$, $uv^{T}=\begin{bmatrix}4&5\\8&10\\12&15\end{bmatrix}$, a rank-1 matrix whose column space is spanned by $u$.

short_description: The matrix exponential exp(A) is the convergent power series sum of A^k/k!; it gives the solution operator e^{tA} of the linear system dx/dt = A x.
formula_plaintext: exp(A) = sum_{k=0}^infty A^k / k! = I + A + A^2/2! + A^3/3! + ...
definition: For a square matrix A ∈ C^{n×n}, the matrix exponential is defined by the power series exp(A) = Σ_{k≥0} A^k/k!, which converges for every A. It satisfies (d/dt) e^{tA} = A e^{tA} = e^{tA} A, so x(t) = e^{tA} x_0 solves dx/dt = A x with x(0) = x_0.
domain_of_definition: All square matrices A ∈ C^{n×n} (equivalently R^{n×n}); the defining series converges absolutely for every A.
range: Invertible matrices: exp(A) ∈ GL(n) with (e^A)^{-1} = e^{-A} and det(e^A) = e^{tr(A)}.
worked_example: If $A$ is diagonalizable as $A = P D P^{-1}$ with $D=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n)$, then $e^{A}=P\,\mathrm{diag}(e^{\lambda_1},\dots,e^{\lambda_n})\,P^{-1}$.

references.title: Horn & Johnson, Topics in Matrix Analysis, Ch. 6

short_description: A vector space V is the direct sum of subspaces U and W when every vector decomposes uniquely as u + w; equivalently V = U + W and U ∩ W = {0}.
formula_latex: V = U \oplus W \iff V = U + W \ \text{and}\ U \cap W = \{0\}
formula_plaintext: V = U ⊕ W  iff  V = U + W and U ∩ W = {0}
definition: A vector space V is the (internal) direct sum of subspaces U and W, written V = U ⊕ W, if V = U + W and U ∩ W = {0}. Equivalently, every v ∈ V has a unique representation v = u + w with u ∈ U and w ∈ W. In finite dimensions this gives dim V = dim U + dim W.
examples: R^2 = span{(1,0)} ⊕ span{(0,1)}: every vector splits uniquely into horizontal and vertical parts.
For any matrix, R^n decomposes as the direct sum of the row space and the null space of the matrix (in R^n with the standard inner product).
The space of n×n matrices is the direct sum of the symmetric and skew-symmetric matrices: M = (M+M^T)/2 + (M-M^T)/2.

references.title: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Sec. 1.C
